methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $148M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $529M - $5.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 22.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: DEEPX
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series C), post-money $529M

data.narrative:
Nearfield Instruments is a Series C semiconductor company founded by Roland van Vliet and Hamed Sadeghian. The valuation analysis triangulates across a comp universe of 3 public semiconductor players (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private frontier-tech peers in chips and semiconductors, including SambaNova Systems, Tenstorrent, Positron AI, and DEEPX.

Two valuation methods are applicable. Stage-progression analysis uses the company's $147.6M total raised as a proxy base value and applies a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical for Series C advancement, yielding $251M. Peer-median analysis across four same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables—SambaNova ($5.0B, Series D), Tenstorrent ($2.7B, Series D), Positron AI ($1.0B, Series B), and DEEPX ($529M, Series C)—produces a median valuation of $1.9B, with a range spanning $529M to $5.0B. Public-comp anchoring via P/S multiples (sector median 22.3x) is inapplicable due to lack of disclosed revenue.

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $213M (low) to $2.1B (high), with a median of $1.1B across two methods. Key uncertainties include the wide dispersion among peer valuations, stage heterogeneity in the comp set, and absence of revenue or profitability data that would enable price-to-sales anchoring. The stage-progression method generates a materially lower floor than peer-median estimates, reflecting methodological sensitivity to capital raised versus market comparables.
